translation.content: They don't make DJs like Lukas Wigflex anymore. The Nottingham DJ and promoter has a love for UK club culture that borders on the unfathomable—and he does it all with gusto that is unmistakably, 100 percent Lukas. Over 19 years, his namesake party series, Wigflex, has grown from a free-bar night in Nottingham with two-for-one pizzas to entice punters into a leading light in the Midlands's nightlife landscape. Wigflex regularly brings contemporary dance music giants like Skee Mask, Aurora Halal and Wata Igarashi to UK shores, all while championing homegrown talent. Recalling its journey from a modest bar night to powerhouse party, he told Stamp The Wax: "I created a mixtape and called it Wigflex 2000. It all just evolved naturally from that." "Evolved naturally" is, perhaps, too modest. In an increasingly hostile operating environment for independent clubs and promoters across the UK, it's hard to overstate the achievement of Wigflex's longevity, a testament to his tenacity and distinctive spirit. Let's be honest, who else could get away with taglining their event: "Survival of the Wrongest." But while he might be the UK's resident wrongun, don't let the tongue-in-cheek persona fool you—he's deadly serious about tunes. His RA Podcast is a window into what Wigflex is all about: electro in all its shades, from the Hauffian to the Drexciyan, alongside wigged-out EBM, quirky head-turners and what Lukas describes as a healthy amount of Wigflex classics from over the years. Even if you didn't know it was coming from him, you would certainly get the impression it sounds a lot like somebody who really, truly adores dance music. What better way to ring in the New Year? – Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? Bitta this, bitta that. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? Recorded at my gaff on a pair of 1210s, a MasterSounds Radius 4V mixer, their accompanying effects unit and a CDJ I borrowed from Ste Roberts to play the couple of unreleased bits in the mix. Musically, I wanted to showcase what my parties and hometown have sounded like over the last 20-odd years. A gentle but intense meander through various psychedelic rudeboy electronics. It's strange, playing music to people is one of the only times my weird little nobhead of a brain doesn't feel the need to rush. I like to be patient and let my records play out. I hope people give the mix time to evolve properly. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Seeing how United develop under Ruben Amorim. The new party I've started with Ivan Smagghe, and playing in Nottingham all night long for the first time in what feels like an eternity. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? Palestine, obviously. Closer to home, the high suicide rates in the UK is pretty sad, we don't even get to see the attempted rates, I can imagine they're even higher. I wish there was more help. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? The Bomb, during my early days of partying, had a huge influence on me and still defines what I look for in the perfect clubbing experience. A mid-sized venue with a great sound system, low ceilings, and plenty of hidden nooks and crannies to explore—without it feeling like a sprawling megaclub. Nowadays, being a resident at Corsica Studios has had a massive impact on how I approach my sets. I’m lucky enough to curate 24-hour or extended parties there, and I've often done long, late-night sets during those weird early morning hours. They give me the freedom to dig deep and take my time. At the last Wigflex, I played for nine hours, and it flew by—it truly felt like home. The sound system there has also made me listen to my records in a whole new way. In my opinion, there aren't many clubs or booths that sound better. In terms of events that had a lasting impact on me, some of my favourites growing up were Border Community at The End and Exodus at the Leeds West Indian Centre. Wigflex began as a small Nottingham party on a Wednesday, and this January marks its 19th anniversary. How have your two roles—promoting and DJing—evolved since you first started? I've loved being on both sides of the booth, it's made me respect promoters and have empathy for how hard the job is and how insane some of them actually are. I've lost everything I've ever owned being a promoter, and tbh, I'd probably do it all again in a heartbeat. As for evolution, I always said I only put on parties to give myself a gig, now that I'm being booked a little more, I'm promoting less, but the parties I do throw are still super special, they keep me grounded and remind me to not take the piss with my rider requests when I'm out and about. What's your opinion on the state of UK club culture at the moment? Given the state of the actual country and what the average person's pockets look like, we're lucky there's any sort of culture still alive. Some of it is super corny, some of it's super inspiring. Big up everyone keeping the dream alive.
translation.blurb: Ring in 2025 with a three-hour flex from a hero of UK club culture.
